title: Forms & Controlled Inputs
summary: Wire form fields to state so React is the single source of truth — the controlled-component pattern.

blocks.md: In plain HTML, an `<input>` keeps its own value in the DOM. In React we usually make inputs **controlled**: their value comes from state, and every keystroke updates that state. React becomes the single source of truth for what's in the field.

blocks.caption: a controlled input

blocks.md: Two bindings make it controlled: `value={name}` (state → input) and `onChange` (input → state). Because the value always mirrors state, you can validate, format, or disable the submit button based on it.

blocks.title: value without onChange = read-only
blocks.md: If you set `value={name}` but forget `onChange`, React locks the field — typing does nothing, and you'll see a console warning. Always pair `value` with an `onChange` (or use `defaultValue` for an uncontrolled field).

blocks.caption: prevent the default reload

blocks.question: What makes an input a *controlled* component?
options: Its value comes from state and changes flow through onChange back into state
blocks.explanation: A controlled input's value is driven by React state (value={...}) and every change updates that state (onChange). React is the source of truth. defaultValue makes an UNcontrolled input.

keyTakeaways: Controlled inputs derive their value from state and update it on every change.
value without onChange makes the field read-only (and warns).
Call `e.preventDefault()` in onSubmit to stop the browser's full-page reload.

exercise.prompt: Build a controlled `<textarea>` whose current character count is shown below it (e.g. '12 characters'). Store the text in state.
exercise.hint: Bind value and onChange to a `text` state, then render `{text.length} characters`.
